# Architecture Decision Document - MMS Metro Mileage Management System

_This document defines the technical architecture for the MMS railway fleet management demo system._

---

## 1. System Overview

### 1.1 Architecture Vision

MMS is a **demo-focused system** designed to win a competitive bid through a 15-minute presentation. The architecture prioritizes:

1. **Demo Stability** - 15 minutes without crashes
2. **Visual Impact** - Fast rendering, smooth interactions
3. **Domain Credibility** - Correct railway terminology, realistic behavior
4. **Technical Proof** - Working solver, real-time recommendations

### 1.2 High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         BROWSER                                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    Next.js 14 App                        │    │
│  │  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐    │    │
│  │  │Dashboard │ │   Map    │ │Allocation│ │ Analysis │    │    │
│  │  │  Page    │ │Component │ │  Page    │ │   Page   │    │    │
│  │  └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘    │    │
│  │                      ↓ HTTP/REST                         │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         SERVER                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                  Fastify API Server                      │    │
│  │  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐    │    │
│  │  │Dashboard │ │  Trains  │ │Allocation│ │Simulation│    │    │
│  │  │  Routes  │ │  Routes  │ │  Routes  │ │  Routes  │    │    │
│  │  └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘    │    │
│  │                      ↓                                   │    │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │  │              Service Layer                       │    │    │
│  │  │  TrainService | AllocationService | SolverClient│    │    │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                    ↓                           ↓                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────────────┐      │
│  │    PostgreSQL 16    │       │   Python Solver Service │      │
│  │  ┌───────────────┐  │       │  ┌───────────────────┐  │      │
│  │  │    trains     │  │       │  │  OR-Tools CP-SAT  │  │      │
│  │  │    depots     │  │       │  │  Recommendation   │  │      │
│  │  │   services    │  │       │  │     Engine        │  │      │
│  │  │  allocations  │  │       │  └───────────────────┘  │      │
│  │  └───────────────┘  │       │         ↑ HTTP          │      │
│  └─────────────────────┘       └─────────────────────────┘      │
│            ↑                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐                                        │
│  │       Redis         │ (session cache, solver results)        │
│  └─────────────────────┘                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 1.3 Key Architectural Decisions

| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Frontend Framework | Next.js 14 (App Router) | SSR for initial load, React ecosystem |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui | Rapid development, consistent design |
| Backend Framework | Fastify | Performance, TypeScript support |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 | Reliable, JSON support, demo-proven |
| Solver | Python + OR-Tools | Best-in-class optimization, justifications |
| State Management | @zazcart/toolkit | Avoids React hook pitfalls |
| API Style | REST | Simplicity for demo scope |

---

## 2. Component Architecture

### 2.1 Frontend Structure

```
src/
├── app/                      # Next.js App Router
│   ├── layout.tsx            # Root layout with nav
│   ├── page.tsx              # Dashboard (home)
│   ├── fleet/
│   │   ├── page.tsx          # Train list
│   │   └── [id]/page.tsx     # Train details
│   ├── allocation/
│   │   └── page.tsx          # Service allocation
│   ├── analysis/
│   │   └── page.tsx          # Km balancing, simulation
│   └── schedule/
│       └── page.tsx          # Maintenance Gantt
│
├── components/
│   ├── ui/                   # shadcn/ui components
│   ├── dashboard/
│   │   ├── KPICard.tsx
│   │   ├── KPIGrid.tsx
│   │   └── DepotCapacity.tsx
│   ├── map/
│   │   ├── FleetMap.tsx
│   │   ├── TrainMarker.tsx
│   │   └── DepotMarker.tsx
│   ├── allocation/
│   │   ├── ServiceList.tsx
│   │   ├── RecommendationCard.tsx
│   │   └── JustificationPanel.tsx
│   ├── analysis/
│   │   ├── DistributionChart.tsx
│   │   ├── ScenarioComparison.tsx
│   │   └── TrainRanking.tsx
│   └── gantt/
│       ├── MaintenanceGantt.tsx
│       └── GanttBlock.tsx
│
├── lib/
│   ├── api.ts                # API client
│   ├── types.ts              # TypeScript interfaces
│   └── utils.ts              # Utilities
│
└── stores/                   # @zazcart/toolkit state
    ├── fleetStore.ts
    ├── allocationStore.ts
    └── simulationStore.ts
```

### 2.2 Backend Structure

```
server/
├── src/
│   ├── index.ts              # Fastify server entry
│   ├── routes/
│   │   ├── dashboard.ts      # GET /api/dashboard/*
│   │   ├── trains.ts         # GET/POST /api/trains/*
│   │   ├── depots.ts         # GET /api/depots/*
│   │   ├── services.ts       # GET /api/services/*
│   │   ├── allocations.ts    # GET/POST /api/allocations/*
│   │   └── simulation.ts     # POST /api/simulation/*
│   │
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── trainService.ts
│   │   ├── depotService.ts
│   │   ├── allocationService.ts
│   │   ├── simulationService.ts
│   │   └── solverClient.ts   # HTTP client to Python solver
│   │
│   ├── db/
│   │   ├── client.ts         # PostgreSQL client (pg or drizzle)
│   │   ├── schema.ts         # Table definitions
│   │   └── seed.ts           # Seed data loader
│   │
│   └── types/
│       └── index.ts          # Shared types
│
└── package.json
```

### 2.3 Solver Structure

```
solver/
├── src/
│   ├── main.py               # FastAPI server
│   ├── models/
│   │   ├── train.py          # Train dataclass
│   │   ├── service.py        # Service dataclass
│   │   └── recommendation.py # Output dataclass
│   │
│   ├── engine/
│   │   ├── allocator.py      # CP-SAT allocation model
│   │   ├── scorer.py         # Train scoring logic
│   │   └── justifier.py      # Justification generator
│   │
│   └── api/
│       └── routes.py         # POST /recommend, POST /simulate
│
├── requirements.txt
└── Dockerfile
```

---

## 3. Data Architecture

### 3.1 Database Schema

```sql
-- Core entities
CREATE TABLE fleets (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    manufacturer VARCHAR(50),
    pm1_interval_km INTEGER NOT NULL,
    pm2_interval_km INTEGER NOT NULL,
    hm_interval_km INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE depots (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    latitude DECIMAL(10, 6),
    longitude DECIMAL(10, 6),
    capacity_tracks INTEGER DEFAULT 5,
    capacity_manhours INTEGER DEFAULT 80,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE trains (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,  -- Business ID: UTE-3400-012
    fleet_id UUID REFERENCES fleets(id),
    base_depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    current_km INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    target_km INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    fitness_status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'fit' CHECK (fitness_status IN ('fit', 'unfit')),
    last_maintenance_date DATE,
    next_maintenance_km INTEGER,
    latitude DECIMAL(10, 6),
    longitude DECIMAL(10, 6),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE services (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    service_code VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,  -- IC 501
    origin VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    destination VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    distance_km INTEGER NOT NULL,
    departure_time TIME NOT NULL,
    arrival_time TIME NOT NULL,
    service_type VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'regional',  -- regional, intercity
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE allocations (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    service_id UUID REFERENCES services(id),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id),
    allocation_date DATE NOT NULL,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending' CHECK (status IN ('pending', 'confirmed', 'completed', 'cancelled')),
    score INTEGER,
    justifications JSONB,  -- Array of reason strings
    created_by VARCHAR(100),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(service_id, allocation_date)
);

-- Mileage history for trends
CREATE TABLE mileage_history (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id),
    recorded_date DATE NOT NULL,
    km_value INTEGER NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Maintenance schedules
CREATE TABLE maintenance_schedules (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    train_id UUID REFERENCES trains(id),
    depot_id UUID REFERENCES depots(id),
    maintenance_type VARCHAR(10) CHECK (maintenance_type IN ('PM1', 'PM2', 'HM', 'OVH')),
    scheduled_date DATE NOT NULL,
    duration_days INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'scheduled' CHECK (status IN ('scheduled', 'in_progress', 'completed', 'cancelled')),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Indexes for performance
CREATE INDEX idx_trains_fleet ON trains(fleet_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_trains_depot ON trains(base_depot_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_allocations_date ON allocations(allocation_date);
CREATE INDEX idx_allocations_status ON allocations(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_mileage_train_date ON mileage_history(train_id, recorded_date);
```

### 3.2 Data Flow

```
┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│   Frontend  │────▶│   API       │────▶│  Database   │
│   (React)   │◀────│  (Fastify)  │◀────│ (PostgreSQL)│
└─────────────┘     └──────┬──────┘     └─────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼
                   ┌─────────────┐
                   │   Solver    │
                   │  (Python)   │
                   └─────────────┘
```

**Read Path (Dashboard):**
1. Frontend requests `/api/dashboard/summary`
2. API queries aggregated KPIs from DB
3. Response cached in Redis (60s TTL)
4. Frontend renders immediately

**Write Path (Allocation):**
1. User selects service, clicks "Recommend"
2. Frontend calls `POST /api/allocations/recommend`
3. API calls Solver service with service details
4. Solver returns ranked recommendations with justifications
5. User confirms, API writes to allocations table
6. Frontend updates optimistically

---

## 4. API Specification

### 4.1 REST Endpoints

#### Dashboard
```
GET /api/dashboard/summary
Response: {
  totalTrains: number,
  availableTrains: number,
  inMaintenanceCount: number,
  kmVariance: number,
  varianceStatus: 'green' | 'yellow' | 'red'
}

GET /api/dashboard/depot-capacity
Response: {
  depots: [{
    id: string,
    name: string,
    code: string,
    currentOccupancy: number,
    maxCapacity: number,
    percentUsed: number
  }]
}
```

#### Trains
```
GET /api/trains
Query: ?fleet=uuid&depot=uuid&status=fit
Response: { trains: Train[] }

GET /api/trains/:id
Response: Train

GET /api/trains/:id/history
Response: { history: MileagePoint[] }
```

#### Allocation
```
GET /api/services/pending
Query: ?date=2025-01-15
Response: { services: Service[] }

POST /api/allocations/recommend
Body: { serviceId: string, date: string }
Response: {
  recommendations: [{
    trainId: string,
    score: number,
    justifications: string[]
  }],
  solverTimeMs: number
}

POST /api/allocations
Body: { serviceId: string, trainId: string, date: string }
Response: { allocation: Allocation }
```

#### Analysis
```
GET /api/balancing/distribution
Query: ?fleet=uuid
Response: {
  average: number,
  variance: number,
  distribution: { kmRange: string, count: number }[],
  outliers: { trainId: string, delta: number }[]
}

POST /api/simulation/create
Body: { name: string, baseDate: string }
Response: { scenarioId: string }

POST /api/simulation/:id/run
Body: { changes: AllocationChange[] }
Response: {
  projectedVariance: number,
  projectedDeadRunning: number,
  comparison: MetricComparison[]
}
```

### 4.2 Solver API (Internal)

```
POST /recommend
Body: {
  service: { id, distance_km, origin, destination },
  candidates: Train[],
  constraints: {
    prioritize_balancing: boolean,
    max_dead_running_km: number
  }
}
Response: {
  recommendations: [{
    train_id: string,
    score: number,
    justifications: string[],
    dead_running_km: number
  }],
  solver_time_ms: number
}
```

---

## 5. Technology Decisions

### 5.1 Frontend Stack

| Technology | Version | Decision Rationale |
|------------|---------|-------------------|
| Next.js | 14.x | App Router, RSC, SSR |
| React | 18.x | Concurrent features, suspense |
| TypeScript | 5.x | Type safety, IDE support |
| Tailwind CSS | 3.x | Utility-first, rapid prototyping |
| shadcn/ui | latest | Pre-built accessible components |
| @zazcart/toolkit | local | State management without React hook issues |
| Recharts | 2.x | Simple charting, React integration |
| Mapbox GL / Leaflet | latest | Interactive maps |

### 5.2 Backend Stack

| Technology | Version | Decision Rationale |
|------------|---------|-------------------|
| Node.js | 20.x LTS | Stable, TypeScript support |
| Fastify | 4.x | Performance, schema validation |
| TypeScript | 5.x | Shared types with frontend |
| PostgreSQL | 16.x | JSONB, reliability |
| Redis | 7.x | Caching, session storage |
| Drizzle ORM | latest | Type-safe SQL, migrations |

### 5.3 Solver Stack

| Technology | Version | Decision Rationale |
|------------|---------|-------------------|
| Python | 3.11 | OR-Tools compatibility |
| FastAPI | 0.100+ | Async, auto-docs |
| OR-Tools | 9.x | Google's optimization suite |
| Pydantic | 2.x | Data validation |
| uvicorn | latest | ASGI server |

---

## 6. Integration Architecture

### 6.1 Frontend ↔ Backend

```
[Next.js App]
     │
     │  fetch('/api/...')
     │  Headers: Content-Type: application/json
     ▼
[Fastify Server]
     │
     │  Route handlers call services
     ▼
[Service Layer]
     │
     │  Drizzle ORM queries
     ▼
[PostgreSQL]
```

**Error Handling:**
- API returns standardized error format: `{ error: string, code: string, details?: object }`
- Frontend uses try/catch with toast notifications
- Network errors show retry option

### 6.2 Backend ↔ Solver

```
[Fastify Service]
     │
     │  HTTP POST (axios/fetch)
     │  Timeout: 30 seconds
     ▼
[Python FastAPI]
     │
     │  CP-SAT model execution
     ▼
[Response with justifications]
```

**Resilience:**
- Solver timeout: 30 seconds hard limit
- Fallback: Return top 3 trains by simple scoring if solver fails
- Cache: Store recent recommendations in Redis (5 min TTL)

---

## 7. Non-Functional Architecture

### 7.1 Performance

| Metric | Target | Implementation |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| Dashboard load | < 2s | SSR + cache |
| API response | < 500ms | Index optimization |
| Map render | < 2s | Tile caching, clustering |
| Solver response | < 30s | CP-SAT with timeout |

### 7.2 Caching Strategy

```
Redis Cache:
├── dashboard:summary (TTL: 60s)
├── trains:list (TTL: 30s)
├── depots:list (TTL: 300s)
├── solver:recommend:{hash} (TTL: 300s)
└── session:{id} (TTL: 3600s)
```

### 7.3 Demo Stability

| Scenario | Mitigation |
|----------|------------|
| Database down | Pre-loaded fallback JSON |
| Solver timeout | Simple ranking fallback |
| Network issue | Service worker cache |
| UI crash | Error boundary with retry |

---

## 8. Development Setup

### 8.1 Monorepo Structure

```
mms/
├── package.json              # Root package (workspaces)
├── apps/
│   ├── web/                  # Next.js frontend
│   └── api/                  # Fastify backend
├── packages/
│   ├── types/                # Shared TypeScript types
│   └── utils/                # Shared utilities
├── solver/                   # Python solver (separate)
├── docker-compose.yml        # Local dev environment
└── scripts/
    ├── seed.ts               # Database seeding
    └── dev.sh                # Start all services
```

### 8.2 Local Development

```bash
# Start infrastructure
docker-compose up -d postgres redis

# Start API
cd apps/api && bun run dev

# Start Solver
cd solver && uvicorn src.main:app --reload

# Start Frontend
cd apps/web && bun run dev
```

### 8.3 Environment Variables

```env
# Database
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://mms:mms@localhost:5432/mms

# Redis
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379

# Solver
SOLVER_URL=http://localhost:8000

# Mapbox (optional, can use Leaflet)
NEXT_PUBLIC_MAPBOX_TOKEN=pk.xxx
```

---

## 9. Security Considerations (Demo Scope)

### 9.1 Demo Simplifications

| Production Need | Demo Approach |
|-----------------|---------------|
| Authentication | None (demo mode) |
| Authorization | None (single user) |
| HTTPS | HTTP for local dev |
| Input validation | Basic schema validation |
| Rate limiting | None |

### 9.2 Data Protection

- No real customer data (seed data only)
- No PII in seed data
- Demo reset between presentations

---

## 10. Deployment Architecture (Demo)

### 10.1 Demo Environment

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Demo Laptop                    │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Docker Compose                    │  │
│  │  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ │  │
│  │  │Next │ │API  │ │Solver│ │PG   │ │  │
│  │  │:3000│ │:4000│ │:8000 │ │:5432│ │  │
│  │  └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                          │
│  Browser → http://localhost:3000        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 10.2 Demo Reset Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Reset demo to clean state
docker-compose down -v
docker-compose up -d
sleep 5
bun run seed
echo "Demo ready!"
```

---

## 11. AI Agent Guidelines

### 11.1 Code Patterns

**DO:**
- Use TypeScript strict mode
- Use Drizzle ORM for database access
- Use @zazcart/toolkit for frontend state
- Use shadcn/ui components
- Follow existing file naming conventions
- Add JSDoc comments for public functions

**DON'T:**
- Use useState for application state (use toolkit)
- Create new component libraries
- Add authentication logic
- Implement features outside PRD scope
- Add time estimates to code comments

### 11.2 File Naming

```
Components: PascalCase.tsx (e.g., TrainCard.tsx)
Utilities: camelCase.ts (e.g., formatKm.ts)
Routes: lowercase/route.ts (e.g., trains/route.ts)
Types: PascalCase.ts (e.g., Train.ts)
```

### 11.3 Testing Priority

1. Solver logic (unit tests)
2. API endpoints (integration tests)
3. Critical UI flows (Playwright)

---

*Document generated by BMAD Architecture Workflow*
*Date: 2025-12-28*

# Story 4.6: Implement Solver Recommendation Engine

## Story Metadata
- **Epic:** 4 - Allocation with Recommendations
- **Status:** done
- **Implemented:** 2025-12-28
- **Session:** SESSION-008

## User Story
As a **system component**,
I want **an OR-Tools CP-SAT optimization engine**,
So that **recommendations are mathematically optimal for km balancing**.

## Acceptance Criteria

| AC | Criteria | Status |
|----|----------|--------|
| AC1 | OR-Tools CP-SAT model implemented | PASS |
| AC2 | Km balancing as primary objective | PASS |
| AC3 | Dead running minimization included | PASS |
| AC4 | Maintenance proximity considered | PASS |
| AC5 | Response time < 30 seconds | PASS |

## Implementation Details

### Files Created
- `apps/solver/engine/cp_sat.py` - Optimization engine

### CP-SAT Model
```python
# Objective weights
KM_BALANCE_WEIGHT = 1000
DEAD_RUNNING_WEIGHT = 100
MAINTENANCE_WEIGHT = 10

# Decision variable: assign[train, service]
# Constraint: one train per service
# Objective: minimize weighted sum
```

### Algorithm
1. Load available trains from database
2. Calculate km deviation from fleet average
3. Calculate Haversine distance for dead running
4. Score maintenance proximity (days until next PM/HM)
5. Solve with CP-SAT, extract top 5
6. Generate justifications from solution

## Dev Notes
CP-SAT provides optimal solutions with provable bounds.
Fallback to simple scoring if solver times out.

## Commit Reference
`38a2379 feat(solver): implement OR-Tools CP-SAT train allocation`

# Story 1.5: Setup Python Solver Service

## Story Metadata
- **Epic:** 1 - Project Foundation & Infrastructure
- **Status:** done
- **Implemented:** 2025-12-28
- **Session:** SESSION-003, SESSION-008

## User Story
As a **developer**,
I want **a Python solver service with OR-Tools**,
So that **the system can generate optimized allocation recommendations**.

## Acceptance Criteria

| AC | Criteria | Status |
|----|----------|--------|
| AC1 | FastAPI server starts on port 8000 | PASS |
| AC2 | /recommend endpoint accepts service request | PASS |
| AC3 | OR-Tools CP-SAT model implemented | PASS |
| AC4 | POST /simulate endpoint exists | PASS |

## Implementation Details

### Files Created
- `apps/solver/main.py` - FastAPI server
- `apps/solver/models/` - Pydantic request/response models
- `apps/solver/engine/cp_sat.py` - CP-SAT optimization engine
- `apps/solver/db/client.py` - Asyncpg PostgreSQL client
- `apps/solver/Dockerfile` - Container configuration
- `apps/solver/requirements.txt` - Python dependencies

### Solver Algorithm
The CP-SAT model optimizes train allocation with weighted objectives:
1. **Km Balancing (weight 1000):** Prioritize under-running trains
2. **Dead Running (weight 100):** Minimize distance from train to service origin
3. **Maintenance Proximity (weight 10):** Factor upcoming maintenance

### Justification Generation
Each recommendation includes 3+ human-readable justifications:
- "Train T-2201 is 15% below fleet average - ideal for balancing"
- "Only 45km dead running from current position"
- "No maintenance scheduled for next 30 days"

### Technical Decisions
- OR-Tools CP-SAT for constraint programming
- Asyncpg for non-blocking PostgreSQL queries
- Haversine formula for distance calculations

## Dev Notes
Initial implementation (SESSION-003) was mock-based.
Full CP-SAT implementation completed in SESSION-008.

## Commit References
- `de902a6 feat: add Sprint Status tracking file`
- `38a2379 feat(solver): implement OR-Tools CP-SAT train allocation`

    16→# Product Requirements Document - MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
    17→
    18→**Author:** MMS_PROGRAMMER
    19→**Date:** 2025-12-28
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Executive Summary
    24→
    25→**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets (>100 units), bridging Operations and Maintenance via predictive, data-driven planning.
    26→
    27→### Vision
    28→
    29→Create a functional demo in **3 months** that:
    30→- Demonstrates technical competence in railway fleet management
    31→- Proves deep understanding of the railway domain
    32→- Impresses decision-makers (Operations Director, client's CTO)
    33→
    34→### Success Metric
    35→
    36→> After 15 minutes of demo, the client thinks: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**
    37→
    38→### Core Problem
    39→
    40→Railway operators face three critical pain points:
    41→1. **Over-running trains** degrade faster, increasing maintenance cost
    42→2. **Under-running trains** represent idle capital
    43→3. **Manual planning in Excel** doesn't scale for 100+ train fleets
    44→
    45→MMS addresses all three points through intelligent mileage balancing, maintenance scheduling, and operations coordination.
    46→
    47→### What Makes This Special
    48→
    49→| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
    50→|----------------|----------------|
    51→| **Km balancing as primary objective** | Competitors treat as secondary feature |
    52→| **Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations interface** | Eliminates silos, reduces rework |
    53→| **Visible dead running cost** | Hidden cost made explicit and optimized |
    54→| **Accessible what-if simulation** | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
    55→| **Visible decision justification** | Trust, not black box - every recommendation explains why |

   232→
   233→### Journey 1: Diana - Allocating a Train to Service
   234→
   235→**The Story:**
   236→Diana arrives at the operations center for her morning shift. The timetable shows that service IC 501 (Lisbon to Porto, 336 km) needs a train assigned for tomorrow's departure. She opens MMS and navigates to the Allocation screen.
   237→
   238→**Scene 1: The Request**
   239→Diana selects service IC 501 from the service list. The system shows the service details: departure at 08:15, expected arrival 11:45, route profile indicating high-speed segments.
   240→
   241→**Scene 2: The Recommendation**
   242→With one click on "Recommend," MMS presents her with a ranked list of 5 suitable trains. The top recommendation, UTE-3400-012, shows a score of 94/100 with clear justifications:
   243→- "Km 12% below fleet average (balancing priority)"
   244→- "Service ends 5km from Porto maintenance depot"
   245→- "PM1 scheduled in 3 days, Porto depot has available slot"
   246→- "Aptitude confirmed for North line"
   247→
   248→**Scene 3: The Decision**
   249→Diana reviews the justifications and appreciates that the system explains WHY this train is recommended. She confirms the allocation with a single click.
   250→
   251→**Scene 4: The Resolution**
   252→The system updates the allocation status, and Diana moves on to the next service. Total time: under 2 minutes, fewer than 5 clicks.
   253→
   254→**Requirements Revealed:**
   255→- Service selection interface
   256→- Recommendation engine with ranking
   257→- Justification display component
   258→- One-click allocation confirmation
   259→- Real-time status updates
   260→
   261→---
   262→
   263→### Journey 2: Paul - Identifying and Resolving Mileage Imbalance
   264→
   265→**The Story:**
   266→Paul starts his Monday morning by checking the fleet health. He's been worried about the mileage variance creeping up over the past few weeks.
   267→
   268→**Scene 1: The Discovery**
   269→Opening the Dashboard, Paul immediately sees the km variance indicator showing 18% in yellow - above the ideal 10% threshold. He clicks on the indicator for details.
   270→
   271→**Scene 2: The Analysis**
   272→The Balancing screen shows a histogram of fleet km distribution. Paul can clearly see 5 trains well above average (over-running) and 4 trains significantly below (under-running). He hovers over train UTE-2200-015 and sees it's 25% below the fleet average.
   273→
   274→**Scene 3: The Simulation**
   275→Paul clicks "New Scenario" to test his hypothesis. He adjusts future allocations to prioritize the under-running trains for high-km services. The simulation runs in under 10 seconds and shows the projected impact: variance dropping from 18% to 14% over the next 30 days.
   276→
   277→**Scene 4: The Comparison**
   278→Using side-by-side comparison, Paul sees that his adjustment reduces variance but increases dead running by 3%. He decides the trade-off is worth it.
   279→
   280→**Scene 5: The Resolution**
   281→Paul applies the scenario changes and documents his decision rationale. Total time: under 10 minutes.
   282→
   283→**Requirements Revealed:**
   284→- Dashboard with variance indicators
   285→- Km distribution visualization (histogram/box plot)
   286→- Train ranking by km delta
   287→- Scenario creation and simulation
   288→- Side-by-side comparison view
   289→- Apply scenario functionality
   290→
   291→---
   292→
   293→### Journey 3: Paul - Planning Maintenance Schedule
   294→
   295→**The Story:**
   296→Paul needs to schedule preventive maintenances for the coming month. He wants to ensure depot capacity isn't exceeded.
   297→
   298→**Scene 1: The Overview**
   299→Paul opens the Maintenance Gantt view showing the next 4 weeks. Each train has a timeline bar, and scheduled maintenances appear as colored blocks (PM1 in light blue, PM2 in dark blue, HM in red).
   300→
   301→**Scene 2: The Conflict**
   302→In week 2, Paul notices the Lisbon depot showing red - capacity at 100%. Three trains are scheduled for PM1 on the same day.
   303→
   304→**Scene 3: The Resolution**
   305→Paul drags one of the PM1 blocks from week 2 to week 3. The system immediately validates: green checkmark - no capacity conflict, train still within maintenance window tolerance.
   306→
   307→**Scene 4: The Confirmation**
   308→Paul saves the change. The system confirms the update and the capacity indicator turns green. Total time: under 5 minutes.
   309→
   310→**Requirements Revealed:**
   311→- Gantt timeline visualization
   312→- Drag-and-drop scheduling
   313→- Real-time conflict validation
   314→- Depot capacity indicators
   315→- Maintenance window tolerance checking
   316→

   321→**The Story:**
   322→Gabriel has 30 minutes before a board meeting. He needs to understand fleet performance for the past month and identify any concerns.
   323→
   324→**Scene 1: The Dashboard**
   325→Gabriel opens MMS on his tablet. The Dashboard immediately shows four KPI cards:

   368→
   369→## Domain Requirements
   370→
   371→### Railway Domain Terminology
   372→
   373→All UI labels and messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology:
   374→
   375→| UI Label | Domain Term | NOT Acceptable |
   376→|----------|-------------|----------------|
   377→| Rolling Stock | Rolling Stock | Vehicle, Train, Asset |
   378→| Fleet | Fleet | Group, Class |
   379→| Depot | Depot | Garage, Workshop |
   380→| Preventive Maintenance | PM1, PM2, HM | Light/Heavy Service |
   381→| Train Fitness | Fitness | Availability |
   382→| Run Assignment | Allocation | Dispatch |
   383→| Dead Running | Dead Running | Empty Movement |
   384→| Service | Service | Route, Trip |
   385→
   386→### Maintenance Hierarchy
   387→
   388→```
   389→Fleet Type
   390→ └─ Maintenance Profile
   391→     ├─ PM1 (every 15,000 km) - Light preventive
   392→     ├─ PM2 (every 45,000 km) - Medium preventive
   393→     ├─ HM (every 90,000 km) - Heavy overhaul
   394→     └─ Overhaul (every 6 years) - Full refurbishment
   395→```
   396→
   397→### Constraint Priority (Solver Must Respect)
   398→
   399→1. **Train Fitness** - Unfit trains never assigned (hard constraint)
   400→2. **Maintenance Windows** - Scheduled maintenance blocks availability
   401→3. **Resource Limits** - Man-hours, track slots per depot
   402→4. **Km Balancing** - Optimize within above constraints
   403→
   404→### Entity Relationships
   405→
   406→```
   407→Fleet (1) ---- (*) Train
   408→  |                 |
   409→  |                 +---- (*) RunAssignment
   410→  |                 |
   411→  |                 +---- (1) TrainFitness [daily state]
   412→  |
   413→  +---- (1) BaseDepot
   414→  |
   415→  +---- (1) MaintenanceProfile
   416→```
   417→

   594→### Seed Data Requirements
   595→
   596→| Entity | Volume | Characteristics |
   597→|--------|--------|-----------------|
   598→| Trains | 100-120 | 4-5 fleet types, distributed mileage |
   599→| Depots | 12-15 | Major Portuguese cities |
   600→| Services | 200-300/day | Regional + Intercity mix |
   601→| History | 6 months | Km progression, allocations |
   602→
   603→---
   604→
   605→## Integration Requirements
   606→
   607→### API Architecture
   608→
   609→```
   610→[Frontend Next.js]
   611→        ↓ REST API
   612→[Backend Fastify/Node.js]
   613→        ↓ JSON over HTTP
   614→[Solver Python/OR-Tools]
   615→```
   616→
   617→### API Endpoints (Demo Scope)
   618→
   619→| Method | Endpoint | Description |
   620→|--------|----------|-------------|
   621→| GET | /api/dashboard/summary | KPI overview |
   622→| GET | /api/trains | List all trains |
   623→| GET | /api/trains/:id | Train details |
   624→| GET | /api/depots | List depots with capacity |
   625→| GET | /api/services/pending | Services needing allocation |
   626→| POST | /api/allocations/recommend | Get recommendations |
   627→| POST | /api/allocations | Confirm allocation |
   628→| GET | /api/balancing/distribution | Km distribution data |
   629→| POST | /api/simulation/create | Create scenario |
   630→| POST | /api/simulation/:id/run | Execute simulation |
   631→| GET | /api/simulation/:id/compare | Compare scenarios |
   632→
   633→### Solver Integration
   634→
   635→**Request:**
   636→```json
   637→{
   638→  "service_id": "uuid",
   639→  "date": "2025-01-15",
   640→  "constraints": {
   641→    "prioritize_balancing": true,
   642→    "max_dead_running_km": 50
   643→  }
   644→}
   645→```
   646→
   647→**Response:**
   648→```json
   649→{
   650→  "recommendations": [
   651→    {
   652→      "train_id": "uuid",
   653→      "score": 94,
   654→      "justifications": [
   655→        "Km 12% below fleet average",
   656→        "Service ends 5km from base depot",
   657→        "PM1 scheduled in 3 days at destination"
   658→      ]
   659→    }
   660→  ],
   661→  "solver_time_ms": 1234
   662→}
   663→```
   664→
   665→---
   666→
   667→## Constraints and Dependencies
   668→
   669→### Technical Constraints
   670→
   671→| Constraint | Impact | Mitigation |
   672→|------------|--------|------------|
   673→| 3-month timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus on P0 only |
   674→| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Seed data, no auth |
   675→| Python solver | Cross-language integration | JSON API |
   676→| OR-Tools CP-SAT | Learning curve | Simplify model |
   677→
   678→### Assumptions
   679→
   680→1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
   681→2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
   682→3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support
   683→4. English UI only (no i18n)
   684→5. Desktop-primary, tablet-secondary (no mobile optimization)
   685→
   686→### Dependencies
   687→
   688→| Dependency | Version | Purpose |
   689→|------------|---------|---------|
   690→| Next.js | 14.x | Frontend framework |
   691→| React | 18.x | UI library |
   692→| Tailwind CSS | 3.x | Styling |
   693→| shadcn/ui | latest | Component library |
   694→| Fastify | 4.x | API server |
   695→| PostgreSQL | 16.x | Database |
   696→| Redis | 7.x | Caching |
   697→| Python | 3.11 | Solver runtime |
   698→| OR-Tools | 9.x | Optimization engine |
   699→| Mapbox/Leaflet | latest | Map visualization |
   700→| Recharts | 2.x | Charts |
   701→
   702→---
   703→
   704→## Appendix: Domain Glossary
   705→
   706→| Term | Definition |
   707→|------|------------|
   708→| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles (trains, carriages) requiring maintenance |
   709→| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile |
   710→| Base Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet |
   711→| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness (fit/unfit) |
   712→| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes |
   713→| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet |
   714→| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations |
   715→| PM1/PM2/HM | Preventive Maintenance levels (light/medium/heavy) |
   716→| EAM | Enterprise Asset Management - external system |
   717→| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
   718→| Overrun | Train exceeds target mileage |
   719→| Underrun | Train falls short of target mileage |
   720→| Variance | Measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
   721→

# Product Requirements Document - MMS - Metro Mileage Management System

**Author:** MMS_PROGRAMMER
**Date:** 2025-12-28

---

## Executive Summary

**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets (>100 units), bridging Operations and Maintenance via predictive, data-driven planning.

### Vision

Create a functional demo in **3 months** that:
- Demonstrates technical competence in railway fleet management
- Proves deep understanding of the railway domain
- Impresses decision-makers (Operations Director, client's CTO)

### Success Metric

> After 15 minutes of demo, the client thinks: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**

### Core Problem

Railway operators face three critical pain points:
1. **Over-running trains** degrade faster, increasing maintenance cost
2. **Under-running trains** represent idle capital
3. **Manual planning in Excel** doesn't scale for 100+ train fleets

MMS addresses all three points through intelligent mileage balancing, maintenance scheduling, and operations coordination.

### What Makes This Special

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| **Km balancing as primary objective** | Competitors treat as secondary feature |
| **Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations interface** | Eliminates silos, reduces rework |
| **Visible dead running cost** | Hidden cost made explicit and optimized |
| **Accessible what-if simulation** | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
| **Visible decision justification** | Trust, not black box - every recommendation explains why |

---

## Project Classification

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Technical Type** | SaaS B2B (Web Application with API Backend + Python Solver) |
| **Domain** | Railway/Transportation Fleet Management |
| **Complexity** | High |
| **Project Context** | Greenfield - new demo for competitive bid |
| **Demo Duration** | 15 minutes |
| **Development Timeline** | 12 weeks |

### Technical Stack Overview

| Layer | Technology |
|-------|------------|
| Frontend | Next.js 14 + React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui |
| Backend | Node.js + Fastify + TypeScript |
| Solver | Python 3.11 + Google OR-Tools (CP-SAT) |
| Database | PostgreSQL 16 |
| Cache | Redis |
| Maps | Mapbox or Leaflet |
| Charts | Recharts or Chart.js |

---

## Success Criteria

### User Success

**Primary Users:** Maintenance Planners, Dispatchers (Operations Controllers), Fleet Managers

| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Planner** | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
| **Planner** | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| **Planner** | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
| **Dispatcher** | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
| **Dispatcher** | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
| **Manager** | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
| **All Users** | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |

**User Success Moments:**
- "I can see the km imbalance immediately" → Dashboard shows variance indicator
- "The system tells me WHY this train" → Justification card explains reasoning
- "I can test my ideas before committing" → What-if simulation works

### Business Success

**Demo Objective:** Win the competitive bid

| Metric | Target | Timeframe |
|--------|--------|-----------|
| Demo Duration | 15 minutes | Presentation day |
| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" | Post-demo |
| Bug-Free Demo | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures | Week 12 |
| Domain Terminology | 100% correct railway terms | Throughout UI |

**Business Milestones:**
- Week 4: Core solver working (proof of technical capability)
- Week 6: Navigable UI with mocked data (visual credibility)
- Week 8: End-to-end flow working (integration proof)
- Week 10: Specialist validation passed (domain credibility)
- Week 12: Demo-ready with fallback screens (risk mitigation)

### Technical Success

| Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|-------------|--------|-----------|
| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in demo |
| Map Update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Interactivity maintained |
| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Presentation reliability |

### Measurable Outcomes

**Quantitative:**
- System handles 80-120 trains, 12-15 depots, 200-300 services/day
- Solver produces 3+ justification reasons per recommendation
- UI uses validated EN 15380 railway terminology
- Km variance visualization shows fleet distribution clearly

**Qualitative:**
- Client perceives depth over breadth ("solving 1 pain point well > 10 poorly")
- Demonstration feels domain-authentic, not generic software
- Decision justifications are understandable by non-technical stakeholders

---

## Product Scope

### MVP - Minimum Viable Product (Must Have)

| Feature | Priority | Justification |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| Dashboard with KPIs | P0 | First impression, executive view |
| Map with train positions | P0 | Visual wow, operational context |
| Km balancing visualization | P0 | Main pain point demonstration |
| Working allocation engine | P0 | Proof of technical capability |
| Recommendation justification | P0 | Trust building, not black box |
| Scenario simulation | P0 | Client interaction, decision support |

### Growth Features (Should Have - Demo Enhancement)

| Feature | Priority | Justification |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| Maintenance Gantt | P1 | Market expectation for planning |
| Visual alerts | P1 | Demonstrates proactivity |
| Depot capacity visualization | P1 | Operational realism |
| Train ranking by km | P1 | Quick identification of outliers |

### Vision (Could Have - Nice for Demo)

| Feature | Priority | Justification |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| PDF/Excel export | P2 | Standard reporting expectation |
| Wheelset balancing | P2 | Advanced domain feature |
| Demo presentation script | P2 | Professional delivery |

### Explicitly Out of Scope (Phase 2+)

| Feature | Why Out | When |
|---------|---------|------|
| MILP optimization (long-term) | Complexity exceeds 3 months | Phase 2 |
| Real EAM integration | Depends on client environment | Phase 2 |
| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
| Native mobile app | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
| Multi-language | English sufficient for demo | Production |

---

## User Journeys

### Personas

#### Paul - The Maintenance Planner

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Name** | Paul, 45 years old |
| **Role** | Maintenance Planner |
| **Goal** | Schedule maintenances minimizing operational impact |
| **Frequency** | Daily, 4-8 hours in system |
| **Device** | Desktop, large monitor |
| **Frustrations** | Excel spreadsheets, lack of visibility, constant rework |
| **Critical Need** | Gantt chart with drag-drop functionality |

#### Diana - The Dispatcher

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Name** | Diana, 38 years old |
| **Role** | Dispatcher (Operations Controller) |
| **Goal** | Allocate trains to services in real-time |
| **Frequency** | Continuous during shift |
| **Device** | Desktop, multiple monitors |
| **Frustrations** | Slow systems, lack of information for quick decisions |
| **Critical Need** | Response time < 3 seconds |

#### Gabriel - The Fleet Manager

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Name** | Gabriel, 52 years old |
| **Role** | Fleet Manager |
| **Goal** | Monitor KPIs, make strategic decisions |
| **Frequency** | Weekly, 30-60 minutes |
| **Device** | Desktop or tablet |
| **Frustrations** | Scattered data across systems, lack of drill-down |
| **Critical Need** | Dashboard with executive view |

---

### Journey 1: Diana - Allocating a Train to Service

**The Story:**
Diana arrives at the operations center for her morning shift. The timetable shows that service IC 501 (Lisbon to Porto, 336 km) needs a train assigned for tomorrow's departure. She opens MMS and navigates to the Allocation screen.

**Scene 1: The Request**
Diana selects service IC 501 from the service list. The system shows the service details: departure at 08:15, expected arrival 11:45, route profile indicating high-speed segments.

**Scene 2: The Recommendation**
With one click on "Recommend," MMS presents her with a ranked list of 5 suitable trains. The top recommendation, UTE-3400-012, shows a score of 94/100 with clear justifications:
- "Km 12% below fleet average (balancing priority)"
- "Service ends 5km from Porto maintenance depot"
- "PM1 scheduled in 3 days, Porto depot has available slot"
- "Aptitude confirmed for North line"

**Scene 3: The Decision**
Diana reviews the justifications and appreciates that the system explains WHY this train is recommended. She confirms the allocation with a single click.

**Scene 4: The Resolution**
The system updates the allocation status, and Diana moves on to the next service. Total time: under 2 minutes, fewer than 5 clicks.

**Requirements Revealed:**
- Service selection interface
- Recommendation engine with ranking
- Justification display component
- One-click allocation confirmation
- Real-time status updates

---

### Journey 2: Paul - Identifying and Resolving Mileage Imbalance

**The Story:**
Paul starts his Monday morning by checking the fleet health. He's been worried about the mileage variance creeping up over the past few weeks.

**Scene 1: The Discovery**
Opening the Dashboard, Paul immediately sees the km variance indicator showing 18% in yellow - above the ideal 10% threshold. He clicks on the indicator for details.

**Scene 2: The Analysis**
The Balancing screen shows a histogram of fleet km distribution. Paul can clearly see 5 trains well above average (over-running) and 4 trains significantly below (under-running). He hovers over train UTE-2200-015 and sees it's 25% below the fleet average.

**Scene 3: The Simulation**
Paul clicks "New Scenario" to test his hypothesis. He adjusts future allocations to prioritize the under-running trains for high-km services. The simulation runs in under 10 seconds and shows the projected impact: variance dropping from 18% to 14% over the next 30 days.

**Scene 4: The Comparison**
Using side-by-side comparison, Paul sees that his adjustment reduces variance but increases dead running by 3%. He decides the trade-off is worth it.

**Scene 5: The Resolution**
Paul applies the scenario changes and documents his decision rationale. Total time: under 10 minutes.

**Requirements Revealed:**
- Dashboard with variance indicators
- Km distribution visualization (histogram/box plot)
- Train ranking by km delta
- Scenario creation and simulation
- Side-by-side comparison view
- Apply scenario functionality

---

### Journey 3: Paul - Planning Maintenance Schedule

**The Story:**
Paul needs to schedule preventive maintenances for the coming month. He wants to ensure depot capacity isn't exceeded.

**Scene 1: The Overview**
Paul opens the Maintenance Gantt view showing the next 4 weeks. Each train has a timeline bar, and scheduled maintenances appear as colored blocks (PM1 in light blue, PM2 in dark blue, HM in red).

**Scene 2: The Conflict**
In week 2, Paul notices the Lisbon depot showing red - capacity at 100%. Three trains are scheduled for PM1 on the same day.

**Scene 3: The Resolution**
Paul drags one of the PM1 blocks from week 2 to week 3. The system immediately validates: green checkmark - no capacity conflict, train still within maintenance window tolerance.

**Scene 4: The Confirmation**
Paul saves the change. The system confirms the update and the capacity indicator turns green. Total time: under 5 minutes.

**Requirements Revealed:**
- Gantt timeline visualization
- Drag-and-drop scheduling
- Real-time conflict validation
- Depot capacity indicators
- Maintenance window tolerance checking

---

### Journey 4: Gabriel - Executive Review

**The Story:**
Gabriel has 30 minutes before a board meeting. He needs to understand fleet performance for the past month and identify any concerns.

**Scene 1: The Dashboard**
Gabriel opens MMS on his tablet. The Dashboard immediately shows four KPI cards:
- Total trains: 112
- Available: 98 (87%)
- In Maintenance: 14
- Km Variance: 18% ⚠️

**Scene 2: The Drill-Down**
The yellow warning on variance catches his attention. He taps it to see the trend chart showing variance increasing over the past 6 months.

**Scene 3: The Context**
Scrolling down, Gabriel sees the depot cards. Two depots (Lisbon and Porto) show near-full capacity. He taps Lisbon depot to see which trains are stationed there.

**Scene 4: The Insight**
Gabriel now has the talking points he needs: "We're seeing increased variance that maintenance is addressing, and Lisbon depot is approaching capacity - we may need to redistribute fleet." Total time: under 15 minutes.

**Requirements Revealed:**
- KPI cards with status indicators
- Trend visualization over time
- Drill-down from summary to details
- Depot capacity visualization
- Mobile-responsive design

---

### Journey Requirements Summary

| Journey | Primary Persona | Key Capabilities Revealed |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Allocate Train | Diana (Dispatcher) | Recommendation engine, justifications, quick confirmation |
| Resolve Imbalance | Paul (Planner) | Distribution charts, simulation, scenario comparison |
| Plan Maintenance | Paul (Planner) | Gantt view, drag-drop, conflict validation |
| Executive Review | Gabriel (Manager) | KPIs, trends, drill-down, mobile responsive |

**Critical Capabilities Identified:**
1. **Recommendation Engine** - Ranked train suggestions with justifications
2. **Km Balancing Visualization** - Distribution charts, variance indicators
3. **Scenario Simulation** - What-if testing with side-by-side comparison
4. **Maintenance Gantt** - Timeline view with drag-drop scheduling
5. **Dashboard KPIs** - Traffic-light status, trend visualization
6. **Drill-Down Navigation** - Fleet → Train → Component → History
7. **Real-Time Validation** - Instant feedback on changes

---

## Domain Requirements

### Railway Domain Terminology

All UI labels and messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology:

| UI Label | Domain Term | NOT Acceptable |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| Rolling Stock | Rolling Stock | Vehicle, Train, Asset |
| Fleet | Fleet | Group, Class |
| Depot | Depot | Garage, Workshop |
| Preventive Maintenance | PM1, PM2, HM | Light/Heavy Service |
| Train Fitness | Fitness | Availability |
| Run Assignment | Allocation | Dispatch |
| Dead Running | Dead Running | Empty Movement |
| Service | Service | Route, Trip |

### Maintenance Hierarchy

```
Fleet Type
 └─ Maintenance Profile
     ├─ PM1 (every 15,000 km) - Light preventive
     ├─ PM2 (every 45,000 km) - Medium preventive
     ├─ HM (every 90,000 km) - Heavy overhaul
     └─ Overhaul (every 6 years) - Full refurbishment
```

### Constraint Priority (Solver Must Respect)

1. **Train Fitness** - Unfit trains never assigned (hard constraint)
2. **Maintenance Windows** - Scheduled maintenance blocks availability
3. **Resource Limits** - Man-hours, track slots per depot
4. **Km Balancing** - Optimize within above constraints

### Entity Relationships

```
Fleet (1) ---- (*) Train
  |                 |
  |                 +---- (*) RunAssignment
  |                 |
  |                 +---- (1) TrainFitness [daily state]
  |
  +---- (1) BaseDepot
  |
  +---- (1) MaintenanceProfile
```

---

## Functional Requirements

### FR-1: Dashboard Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-1.1 | Display fleet summary KPIs | P0 | 4 KPI cards: total, available, in maintenance, variance |
| FR-1.2 | Show variance indicator with traffic-light | P0 | Green ≤10%, Yellow 10-20%, Red >20% |
| FR-1.3 | Display depot capacity cards | P1 | Each depot shows current/max capacity |
| FR-1.4 | Show maintenance alerts | P1 | Upcoming PM/HM in next 7 days |
| FR-1.5 | Enable drill-down navigation | P0 | Click any element to see details |

### FR-2: Map Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-2.1 | Display train positions on map | P0 | All active trains visible as markers |
| FR-2.2 | Color-code by status | P0 | Available=green, In-service=blue, Maintenance=red |
| FR-2.3 | Show depot locations | P1 | Depot icons with capacity indicator |
| FR-2.4 | Enable train selection | P0 | Click marker shows train card |
| FR-2.5 | Auto-refresh positions | P1 | Update every 30 seconds |

### FR-3: Allocation Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-3.1 | List pending services | P0 | Services needing train assignment |
| FR-3.2 | Generate train recommendations | P0 | Ranked list with scores |
| FR-3.3 | Display justifications | P0 | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| FR-3.4 | Confirm allocation | P0 | One-click assign |
| FR-3.5 | Filter trains by criteria | P1 | By fleet, depot, km range |

### FR-4: Balancing Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-4.1 | Visualize km distribution | P0 | Histogram or box plot |
| FR-4.2 | Rank trains by delta | P0 | Sorted list: most over/under |
| FR-4.3 | Show fleet average | P0 | Reference line on chart |
| FR-4.4 | Identify outliers | P1 | Highlight >15% deviation |

### FR-5: Simulation Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-5.1 | Create what-if scenario | P0 | Clone current state to sandbox |
| FR-5.2 | Modify future allocations | P0 | Reassign trains in scenario |
| FR-5.3 | Run simulation | P0 | Calculate projected impact |
| FR-5.4 | Compare scenarios | P0 | Side-by-side metrics |
| FR-5.5 | Apply scenario | P1 | Commit changes to live plan |

### FR-6: Maintenance Gantt Module

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-6.1 | Display timeline view | P1 | 4-week view, trains as rows |
| FR-6.2 | Show maintenance blocks | P1 | Color-coded PM1/PM2/HM |
| FR-6.3 | Enable drag-drop | P1 | Move maintenance to different date |
| FR-6.4 | Validate constraints | P1 | Real-time conflict checking |
| FR-6.5 | Show depot capacity | P1 | Aggregate view per depot/week |

### FR-7: Recommendation Engine (Solver)

| ID | Requirement | Priority | Acceptance Criteria |
|----|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| FR-7.1 | Accept service request | P0 | Service ID, date, route profile |
| FR-7.2 | Filter available trains | P0 | Exclude unfit, in-maintenance |
| FR-7.3 | Score by balancing criteria | P0 | Prioritize under-running |
| FR-7.4 | Consider dead running | P1 | Factor proximity to depot |
| FR-7.5 | Generate justifications | P0 | 3+ human-readable reasons |
| FR-7.6 | Return ranked list | P0 | Top 5 recommendations |

---

## Non-Functional Requirements

### NFR-1: Performance

| ID | Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|----|-------------|--------|-----------|
| NFR-1.1 | Allocation recommendation time | < 30 seconds | Dispatcher patience |
| NFR-1.2 | Screen loading | < 3 seconds | Demo credibility |
| NFR-1.3 | Map refresh | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
| NFR-1.4 | Simulation response | < 10 seconds | Interactive feel |
| NFR-1.5 | Database queries | < 500ms | Responsive UI |

### NFR-2: Scalability

| ID | Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|----|-------------|--------|-----------|
| NFR-2.1 | Trains supported | 80-120 | Demo fleet size |
| NFR-2.2 | Depots supported | 12-15 | Network coverage |
| NFR-2.3 | Daily services | 200-300 | Typical operation |
| NFR-2.4 | Concurrent users | 10-20 | Demo scenario |

### NFR-3: Reliability

| ID | Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|----|-------------|--------|-----------|
| NFR-3.1 | Demo stability | 15 min no crash | Presentation length |
| NFR-3.2 | Data consistency | No conflicts | Trust |
| NFR-3.3 | Fallback screens | Available | Risk mitigation |

### NFR-4: Usability

| ID | Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|----|-------------|--------|-----------|
| NFR-4.1 | Allocation flow | < 2 min, < 5 clicks | Efficiency |
| NFR-4.2 | Learning curve | 5 min orientation | Demo scenario |
| NFR-4.3 | Domain terminology | 100% EN 15380 | Credibility |

---

## Data Requirements

### Core Entities

#### trains
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| id | UUID | Primary key |
| train_id | VARCHAR(20) | Business identifier (e.g., UTE-3400-012) |
| fleet_type | VARCHAR(50) | Fleet classification |
| base_depot_id | UUID | Home depot FK |
| current_km | INTEGER | Cumulative mileage |
| target_km | INTEGER | Expected mileage for period |
| fitness_status | ENUM | fit/unfit |
| last_maintenance | DATE | Last PM completion |
| next_maintenance_km | INTEGER | Trigger for next PM |

#### fleets
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| id | UUID | Primary key |
| name | VARCHAR(50) | Fleet name (e.g., Metro Series 2200) |
| manufacturer | VARCHAR(50) | Alstom, Siemens, etc. |
| pm1_interval_km | INTEGER | PM1 every X km |
| pm2_interval_km | INTEGER | PM2 every X km |
| hm_interval_km | INTEGER | HM every X km |

#### depots
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| id | UUID | Primary key |
| name | VARCHAR(100) | Depot name |
| code | VARCHAR(10) | Short code |
| latitude | DECIMAL | Location |
| longitude | DECIMAL | Location |
| capacity_tracks | INTEGER | Maintenance tracks |
| capacity_manhours | INTEGER | Daily man-hours available |

#### services
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| id | UUID | Primary key |
| service_code | VARCHAR(20) | Service identifier |
| origin | VARCHAR(100) | Departure station |
| destination | VARCHAR(100) | Arrival station |
| distance_km | INTEGER | Route length |
| departure_time | TIME | Scheduled departure |
| arrival_time | TIME | Scheduled arrival |

#### allocations
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| id | UUID | Primary key |
| service_id | UUID | Service FK |
| train_id | UUID | Assigned train FK |
| allocation_date | DATE | Date of service |
| status | ENUM | pending/confirmed/completed |
| justifications | JSONB | Array of reason strings |
| created_by | UUID | User FK |
| created_at | TIMESTAMP | Allocation timestamp |

### Seed Data Requirements

| Entity | Volume | Characteristics |
|--------|--------|-----------------|
| Trains | 100-120 | 4-5 fleet types, distributed mileage |
| Depots | 12-15 | Major Portuguese cities |
| Services | 200-300/day | Regional + Intercity mix |
| History | 6 months | Km progression, allocations |

---

## Integration Requirements

### API Architecture

```
[Frontend Next.js]
        ↓ REST API
[Backend Fastify/Node.js]
        ↓ JSON over HTTP
[Solver Python/OR-Tools]
```

### API Endpoints (Demo Scope)

| Method | Endpoint | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| GET | /api/dashboard/summary | KPI overview |
| GET | /api/trains | List all trains |
| GET | /api/trains/:id | Train details |
| GET | /api/depots | List depots with capacity |
| GET | /api/services/pending | Services needing allocation |
| POST | /api/allocations/recommend | Get recommendations |
| POST | /api/allocations | Confirm allocation |
| GET | /api/balancing/distribution | Km distribution data |
| POST | /api/simulation/create | Create scenario |
| POST | /api/simulation/:id/run | Execute simulation |
| GET | /api/simulation/:id/compare | Compare scenarios |

### Solver Integration

**Request:**
```json
{
  "service_id": "uuid",
  "date": "2025-01-15",
  "constraints": {
    "prioritize_balancing": true,
    "max_dead_running_km": 50
  }
}
```

**Response:**
```json
{
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "train_id": "uuid",
      "score": 94,
      "justifications": [
        "Km 12% below fleet average",
        "Service ends 5km from base depot",
        "PM1 scheduled in 3 days at destination"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "solver_time_ms": 1234
}
```

---

## Constraints and Dependencies

### Technical Constraints

| Constraint | Impact | Mitigation |
|------------|--------|------------|
| 3-month timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus on P0 only |
| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Seed data, no auth |
| Python solver | Cross-language integration | JSON API |
| OR-Tools CP-SAT | Learning curve | Simplify model |

### Assumptions

1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support
4. English UI only (no i18n)
5. Desktop-primary, tablet-secondary (no mobile optimization)

### Dependencies

| Dependency | Version | Purpose |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Next.js | 14.x | Frontend framework |
| React | 18.x | UI library |
| Tailwind CSS | 3.x | Styling |
| shadcn/ui | latest | Component library |
| Fastify | 4.x | API server |
| PostgreSQL | 16.x | Database |
| Redis | 7.x | Caching |
| Python | 3.11 | Solver runtime |
| OR-Tools | 9.x | Optimization engine |
| Mapbox/Leaflet | latest | Map visualization |
| Recharts | 2.x | Charts |

---

## Appendix: Domain Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles (trains, carriages) requiring maintenance |
| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile |
| Base Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet |
| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness (fit/unfit) |
| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes |
| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet |
| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations |
| PM1/PM2/HM | Preventive Maintenance levels (light/medium/heavy) |
| EAM | Enterprise Asset Management - external system |
| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
| Overrun | Train exceeds target mileage |
| Underrun | Train falls short of target mileage |
| Variance | Measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |

---

*Document generated by BMAD PRD Workflow*
*Date: 2025-12-28*

content.content:
1→/**
     2→ * MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
     3→ * Database Schema using Drizzle ORM
     4→ *
     5→ * Based on Architecture Decision Document (2025-12-28)
     6→ */
     7→
     8→import {
     9→  pgTable,
    10→  uuid,
    11→  varchar,
    12→  integer,
    13→  decimal,
    14→  timestamp,
    15→  date,
    16→  time,
    17→  jsonb,
    18→  index,
    19→  uniqueIndex,
    20→} from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
    21→import { relations } from 'drizzle-orm';
    22→
    23→// =============================================================================
    24→// Fleets Table
    25→// =============================================================================
    26→export const fleets = pgTable('fleets', {
    27→  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
    28→  name: varchar('name', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
    29→  manufacturer: varchar('manufacturer', { length: 50 }),
    30→  pm1IntervalKm: integer('pm1_interval_km').notNull(),
    31→  pm2IntervalKm: integer('pm2_interval_km').notNull(),
    32→  hmIntervalKm: integer('hm_interval_km').notNull(),
    33→  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
    34→});
    35→
    36→// =============================================================================
    37→// Depots Table
    38→// =============================================================================
    39→export const depots = pgTable(
    40→  'depots',
    41→  {
    42→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
    43→    name: varchar('name', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
    44→    code: varchar('code', { length: 10 }).notNull(),
    45→    latitude: decimal('latitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
    46→    longitude: decimal('longitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
    47→    capacityTracks: integer('capacity_tracks').default(5),
    48→    capacityManhours: integer('capacity_manhours').default(80),
    49→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
    50→  },
    51→  (table) => [
    52→    uniqueIndex('depots_code_idx').on(table.code),
    53→  ]
    54→);
    55→
    56→// =============================================================================
    57→// Trains Table
    58→// =============================================================================
    59→export const trains = pgTable(
    60→  'trains',
    61→  {
    62→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
    63→    trainId: varchar('train_id', { length: 20 }).notNull(), // Business ID: UTE-3400-012
    64→    fleetId: uuid('fleet_id').references(() => fleets.id),
    65→    baseDepotId: uuid('base_depot_id').references(() => depots.id),
    66→    currentKm: integer('current_km').default(0),
    67→    targetKm: integer('target_km').default(0),
    68→    fitnessStatus: varchar('fitness_status', { length: 10 }).default('fit'),
    69→    lastMaintenanceDate: date('last_maintenance_date'),
    70→    nextMaintenanceKm: integer('next_maintenance_km'),
    71→    latitude: decimal('latitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
    72→    longitude: decimal('longitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
    73→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
    74→    updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at').defaultNow(),
    75→  },
    76→  (table) => [
    77→    uniqueIndex('trains_train_id_idx').on(table.trainId),
    78→    index('trains_fleet_idx').on(table.fleetId),
    79→    index('trains_depot_idx').on(table.baseDepotId),
    80→  ]
    81→);
    82→
    83→// =============================================================================
    84→// Services Table
    85→// =============================================================================
    86→export const services = pgTable(
    87→  'services',
    88→  {
    89→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
    90→    serviceCode: varchar('service_code', { length: 20 }).notNull(), // IC 501
    91→    origin: varchar('origin', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
    92→    destination: varchar('destination', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
    93→    distanceKm: integer('distance_km').notNull(),
    94→    departureTime: time('departure_time').notNull(),
    95→    arrivalTime: time('arrival_time').notNull(),
    96→    serviceType: varchar('service_type', { length: 20 }).default('regional'),
    97→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
    98→  },
    99→  (table) => [
   100→    uniqueIndex('services_code_idx').on(table.serviceCode),
   101→  ]
   102→);
   103→
   104→// =============================================================================
   105→// Allocations Table
   106→// =============================================================================
   107→export const allocations = pgTable(
   108→  'allocations',
   109→  {
   110→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   111→    serviceId: uuid('service_id').references(() => services.id),
   112→    trainId: uuid('train_id').references(() => trains.id),
   113→    allocationDate: date('allocation_date').notNull(),
   114→    status: varchar('status', { length: 20 }).default('pending'),
   115→    score: integer('score'),
   116→    justifications: jsonb('justifications').$type<string[]>(),
   117→    createdBy: varchar('created_by', { length: 100 }),
   118→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
   119→  },
   120→  (table) => [
   121→    uniqueIndex('allocations_service_date_idx').on(table.serviceId, table.allocationDate),
   122→    index('allocations_date_idx').on(table.allocationDate),
   123→    index('allocations_status_idx').on(table.status),
   124→  ]
   125→);
   126→
   127→// =============================================================================
   128→// Mileage History Table
   129→// =============================================================================
   130→export const mileageHistory = pgTable(
   131→  'mileage_history',
   132→  {
   133→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   134→    trainId: uuid('train_id').references(() => trains.id),
   135→    recordedDate: date('recorded_date').notNull(),
   136→    kmValue: integer('km_value').notNull(),
   137→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
   138→  },
   139→  (table) => [
   140→    index('mileage_train_date_idx').on(table.trainId, table.recordedDate),
   141→  ]
   142→);
   143→
   144→// =============================================================================
   145→// Maintenance Schedules Table
   146→// =============================================================================
   147→export const maintenanceSchedules = pgTable(
   148→  'maintenance_schedules',
   149→  {
   150→    id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
   151→    trainId: uuid('train_id').references(() => trains.id),
   152→    depotId: uuid('depot_id').references(() => depots.id),
   153→    maintenanceType: varchar('maintenance_type', { length: 10 }).notNull(), // PM1, PM2, HM, OVH
   154→    scheduledDate: date('scheduled_date').notNull(),
   155→    durationDays: integer('duration_days').default(1),
   156→    status: varchar('status', { length: 20 }).default('scheduled'),
   157→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
   158→  },
   159→  (table) => [
   160→    index('maintenance_train_idx').on(table.trainId),
   161→    index('maintenance_depot_idx').on(table.depotId),
   162→    index('maintenance_date_idx').on(table.scheduledDate),
   163→  ]
   164→);
   165→
   166→// =============================================================================
   167→// Relations
   168→// =============================================================================
   169→
   170→export const fleetsRelations = relations(fleets, ({ many }) => ({
   171→  trains: many(trains),
   172→}));
   173→
   174→export const depotsRelations = relations(depots, ({ many }) => ({
   175→  trains: many(trains),
   176→  maintenanceSchedules: many(maintenanceSchedules),
   177→}));
   178→
   179→export const trainsRelations = relations(trains, ({ one, many }) => ({
   180→  fleet: one(fleets, {
   181→    fields: [trains.fleetId],
   182→    references: [fleets.id],
   183→  }),
   184→  baseDepot: one(depots, {
   185→    fields: [trains.baseDepotId],
   186→    references: [depots.id],
   187→  }),
   188→  allocations: many(allocations),
   189→  mileageHistory: many(mileageHistory),
   190→  maintenanceSchedules: many(maintenanceSchedules),
   191→}));
   192→
   193→export const servicesRelations = relations(services, ({ many }) => ({
   194→  allocations: many(allocations),
   195→}));
   196→
   197→export const allocationsRelations = relations(allocations, ({ one }) => ({
   198→  service: one(services, {
   199→    fields: [allocations.serviceId],
   200→    references: [services.id],
   201→  }),
   202→  train: one(trains, {
   203→    fields: [allocations.trainId],
   204→    references: [trains.id],
   205→  }),
   206→}));
   207→
   208→export const mileageHistoryRelations = relations(mileageHistory, ({ one }) => ({
   209→  train: one(trains, {
   210→    fields: [mileageHistory.trainId],
   211→    references: [trains.id],
   212→  }),
   213→}));
   214→
   215→export const maintenanceSchedulesRelations = relations(maintenanceSchedules, ({ one }) => ({
   216→  train: one(trains, {
   217→    fields: [maintenanceSchedules.trainId],
   218→    references: [trains.id],
   219→  }),
   220→  depot: one(depots, {
   221→    fields: [maintenanceSchedules.depotId],
   222→    references: [depots.id],
   223→  }),
   224→}));
   225→
